Strong gradient winds howled across the area during the morning and early afternoon hours.  Widespread and significant damage was done to trees and power lines.  Isolated roof and other structural damage also occurred along with several reports of semi-trucks being blow off roads.  One Quad City roofer was quoted as saying:  "I've been a roofer for 27 years and I've never experienced anything like this."  Reports indicated damage was done mostly to new roofs whose shingles had not completely sealed.Gusts at specific airport locations:  Burlington, Iowa 47 knots, Cedar Rapids, Iowa 50 knots, Clinton, Iowa 57 knots, Dubuque, Iowa 48 knots, Davenport, Iowa 59 knots, Iowa City, Iowa 46 knots.

Spring snowmelt from very heavy snow cover across the upper basin brought significant flooding to portions of the Mississippi River.  Several locations reached the third through fifth highest crests of record.  Crests might have been higher; however, precipitation for March and April over Iowa and Illinois was well below normal, therefore the area tributary rivers contributed very little to the crest.  Economic impacts were significant; however, because the initial forecasts from the River Forecast Center were quite good, most of the expenditures were spent on preventative measures rather than on flood repairs and replacement.  The primary costs were from sandbagging, and interupted commerce, rather than in water damage.  Barge navigation on the Mississippi River and train traffic along the river was suspended for up to two weeks.  Cities with flood wall or levee protection faired quite well with little impact.  Among those without protection; Davenport, Iowa, reported the cost in city employee overtime and sandbagging to to be at $420,000.  This cost however, did not cover any of the loss of business to the downtown area where one business reported losses of $200,000 a day.

An upper level low pressure system moved into the southern Rockies and advanced northeast into the midwest.  As is often the case during an El Nino winter, there was little cold air in place over Iowa.  Even though the surface low passed well to the south of the state, leaving Iowa in what would normally be the snow area of the system, precipitation took the form of freezing rain.  Freezing rain and sleet moved into the state during the afternoon on the 21st and spread northeast slowly.  The heaviest accumulation of ice was over the south third of the state with ice accretion of one to two tenths of an inch.  Amounts were generally under one tenth inch farther north.  During the early morning hours of the 22nd, the precipitation changed to light snow for a period of time as the upper low passed over the state.  Damage from the storm was relatively minor with spotty reports of power outages and broken tree limbs.  Many of the tree limbs fell due to the fact they had been weakened by several very wet snows earlier.  Road conditions deteriorated as the freezing rain and sleet advanced north.  Though no major travel problems were reported, there were hundreds of reports of cars slipping into ditches.

An intense upper level low pressure swung northeast across the central U.S. on the 24, bringing a swath of heavy snow across Iowa.  A band of five to eight inch snow fell from south central into southeast Iowa during the day Christmas Eve.  Some freezing rain was mixed with the snow, however the icing was not a serious problem.  Some of the heavier snowfall included 8 inches at Ottumwa, along with several 8 inch reports along the southern tier of counties from Ottumwa west to south of Creston.  Seven inches was received in Chariton, with six to eight inches recorded in Poweshiek County.  The snow made travel difficult as the consistency of the snow was of the heavy wet variety.  Roads were slushy and gradually became snow covered during the late afternoon hours.

An upper level low pressure system moved into the southern Rockies and advanced northeast into the midwest.  As is often the case during an El Nino winter, there was little cold air in place over Iowa.  Even though the surface low passed well to the south of the state, leaving Iowa in what would normally be the snow area of the system, precipitation took the form of freezing rain.  Freezing rain and sleet moved into the state during the afternoon on the 21st and spread northeast slowly.  The heaviest accumulation of ice was over the south third of the state with ice accretion of one to two tenths of an inch.  Amounts were generally under one tenth inch farther north.  During the early morning hours of the 22nd, the precipitation changed to light snow for a period of time as the upper low passed over the state.  Damage from the storm was relatively minor with spotty reports of power outages and broken tree limbs.  Many of the tree limbs fell due to the fact they had been weakened by several very wet snows earlier.  Road conditions deteriorated as the freezing rain and sleet advanced north.  Though no major travel problems were reported, there were hundreds of reports of cars slipping into ditches.

A strong storm system passed to the south of Iowa during the 3rd and left a light dusting of snow.  The upper air flow became strong cyclonic northwest.  An Alberta clipper type storm system moved southeast with the central crossing the southwest corner of the state.  Much of the state received from one to four inches of snow.  A narrow band of heavy snow fell over south central Iowa.  Amounts in the band were in the 5 to 8 inch range.  A narrow band extended from Derby toward Lake Rathbun with the heaviest snowfall reported at Derby where 8 inches fell.  The snow caused travel problems statewide as the ground temperatures were above freezing at the onset of the snow.  Snow melted on the roads and froze after nightfall.  The ice persisted into the morning of the 5th and caused a great deal of traffic problems.

An intense upper level low pressure swung northeast across the central U.S. on the 24, bringing a swath of heavy snow across Iowa.  A band of five to eight inch snow fell from south central into southeast Iowa during the day Christmas Eve.  Some freezing rain was mixed with the snow, however the icing was not a serious problem.  Some of the heavier snowfall included 8 inches at Ottumwa, along with several 8 inch reports along the southern tier of counties from Ottumwa west to south of Creston.  Seven inches was received in Chariton, with six to eight inches recorded in Poweshiek County.  The snow made travel difficult as the consistency of the snow was of the heavy wet variety.  Roads were slushy and gradually became snow covered during the late afternoon hours.

As is often the case during an El Nino winter, there was little cold air in place over Iowa.  Even though the surface low passed well to the south of the state, leaving Iowa in what would normally be the snow area of the system, precipitation took the form of freezing rain.  Freezing rain and sleet moved into the state during the afternoon on the 21st and spread northeast slowly.  The heaviest accumulation of ice was over the south third of the state with ice accretion of one to two tenths of an inch.  Amounts were generally under one tenth inch farther north.  During the early morning hours of the 22nd, the precipitation changed to light snow for a period of time as the upper low passed over the state.  Damage from the storm was relatively minor with spotty reports of power outages and broken tree limbs.  Many of the tree limbs fell due to the fact they had been weakened by several very wet snows earlier.  Road conditions deteriorated as the freezing rain and sleet advanced north.  Though no major travel problems were reported, there were hundreds of reports of cars slipping into ditches.

An intense upper level low pressure swung northeast across the central U.S. on the 24, bringing a swath of heavy snow across Iowa.  A band of five to eight inch snow fell from south central into southeast Iowa during the day Christmas Eve.  Some freezing rain was mixed with the snow, however the icing was not a serious problem.  Some of the heavier snowfall included 8 inches at Ottumwa, along with several 8 inch reports along the southern tier of counties from Ottumwa west to south of Creston.  Seven inches was received in Chariton, with six to eight inches recorded in Poweshiek County.  The snow made travel difficult as the consistency of the snow was of the heavy wet variety.  Roads were slushy and gradually became snow covered during the late afternoon hours.

A winter storm dumped a swath of 3 to 6 inch snowfalls across northeast Iowa and west central Illinois.  A narrow band of heavier snow produced localized amounts ranging from 6 inches in Clinton and Jackson counties to 8 inches in northern Linn county with an isolated maximum of 10 inches in northern Scott county.

Thhunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage, which resulted in scattered damage to cars.

Cold air spilled south into Iowa as a cold polar vortex aloft dropped south into the state.  Snow fell for an extended period of snow occurred, however much of it fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 14th into the early morning hours of the 15th.  All of the state received snow with the heaviest over the northeast half of the state.  Amounts greater than six inches occurred over the north central counties, extending into the Waterloo area.  The heaviest snowfall report came from Butler County, where an observer in Allison reported 12 inches of snow.  Most of the snow in the heavy snow area was in the 6 to 7 inch range however.  Travel was difficult due to blowing snow and the fact that the snow initially melted on road surfaces and then froze.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 30 MPH range through the storm, causing greatly reduced visibilities in open areas.

Intense low pressure moved into central Canada During the early morning hours of the 13th.  A strong cold front swept through Iowa as the low lifted north.  High winds swept in as the cold air overspread the state.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 MPH were common with frequent gusts to between 50 to 60 MPH.  Scattered damage was reported over the west half of the state.  The only significant reports came from the north central counties, specifically Humboldt County.  Winds were recorded at 81 MPH near Rutland at 0347 CST.  These winds caused damage around $47,000 to a greenhouse there.  Thirty thousand dollars damage was to plants and produce.

Iowa Gov. Terry Branstad requested disaster aid for 52 counties after an early season snowstorm dumped several inches of snow across the state.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported across the area with at least 2  serious accidents and several ditched vehicles. In Buchanan County several minor injuries were reported when a  bus went off Highway 20. One woman in Cedar Rapids was treated for injuries after she was struck by a falling tree branch.  Heavy snow downed power lines and trees and left about 120,000 persons without power across the area.  Rural Washington County residents were without 911 and long distance telephone service much of the day.  The storm closed the Cedar Rapids Airport stranding travellers for 5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm of the season developed over the central Rockies as a powerful upper level jet stream dove into the southern Rockies.  Intense low pressure developed over northern Oklahoma, then drifted northeast on the early morning of the 26th.  A cold high pressure of Arctic origin was poised to the north of Iowa.  The high gradually pushed cold air south into the central U.S. causing the precipitation on the northwest edge of the storm system to change to snow.  During the early morning hours, the system went convective with widespread "thundersnow" reported over the south half of Iowa.  Lightning struck a tree outside of a house in Nevada causing damage to electrical appliances as well as telephone damage in the house.  The "thundersnow" resulted in very heavy snowfall with a band of nine inch or greater snowfall extending from Ringgold County in the south central to Mahaska County in the southeast part of the state.  Six inches or greater fell in a several hour period over much of the southeast half of the state.  The heaviest snow was recorded in Knoxville, with 11.5 inches.  Beaconsfield came close behind with 10 inches recorded.  There was an unofficial report of 11 inches from Adair County as well.  Since the leaves were still on the trees, damage was extensive.  Numerous trees were damaged, by some estimates at least 75% of the trees.  Falling branches caused by the weight of the heavy snow felled power lines across the state.  MidAmerica Energy reported 100,000 customers were without electrical service at the height of the storm.  Fifty thousand were in the City of Des Moines.  In addition, REC's indicated another 2,000 to 3,000 people without electricity.  Damage to lines and electrical service was estimated at over $10 million.  Damage was extensive to telephone and cable TV lines as well.  Damage from those utilities was several million dollars each.  Tree damage was more difficult to assess, but was certainly several million dollars as well.  Crop damage was widespread as soybean crops in the field were virtually destroyed by the heavy wet snow.  The corn crop fared better though some lodging occurred.  At the time of the storm, 6% of the soybean crop and 51% of the corn crop remained in the field.  Damage estimates were placed at $24 million on the beans, and $41 million on the corn crop.  Following the storm, Iowa Governor Terry Branstad declared 52 Counties disaster areas.  This included Grundy, Black Hawk, Story, Marshall, Tama, Guthrie, Dallas, Polk, Jasper, Poweshiek, Cass, Adair, Madison, Warren, Marion, Mahaska, Adams, Union, Clarke, Lucas, Monroe, Wapello, Taylor, Ringgold, Decatur, Wayne, Appanoose, and Davis Counties.  Subsequently, 13 Iowa counties were declared Federal Disaster Areas.  These included Clarke, Jasper, Madison, Mahaska, Marion, Polk, Tama, Union, and Warrne Counties within the Des Moines County Warning Area.  Clean-up costs for the 13 counties was estimated at $5.1 million, ranging from $30,000 in Tama County to $1.84 million in Polk County.

Gradient winds caused widespread damage to trees and property in Iowa and Illinois.  Over 65,000 power customers were without power for several days due to downed power lines. A large cottonwood tree fell on a home in Camanche causing serious structural damage.  The winds slowed river operations at Lock and Dam 13 near Fulton IL as some boaters were afraid to leave the safety of the narrow channel and enter the wide open upstream pool where winds might capsize their boats.

A winter storm dumped a swath of 3 to 6 inch snowfalls across northeast Iowa and west central Illinois.  A narrow band of heavier snow produced localized amounts ranging from 6 inches in Clinton and Jackson counties to 8 inches in northern Linn county with an isolated maximum of 10 inches in northern Scott county.

Cold air spilled south into Iowa as a cold polar vortex aloft dropped south into the state.  Snow fell for an extended period of snow occurred, however much of it fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 14th into the early morning hours of the 15th.  All of the state received snow with the heaviest over the northeast half of the state.  Amounts greater than six inches occurred over the north central counties, extending into the Waterloo area.  The heaviest snowfall report came from Butler County, where an observer in Allison reported 12 inches of snow.  Most of the snow in the heavy snow area was in the 6 to 7 inch range however.  Travel was difficult due to blowing snow and the fact that the snow initially melted on road surfaces and then froze.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 30 MPH range through the storm, causing greatly reduced visibilities in open areas.

The first major winter storm of the season developed over the central Rockies as a powerful upper level jet stream dove into the southern Rockies.  Intense low pressure developed over northern Oklahoma, then drifted northeast on the early morning of the 26th.  A cold high pressure of Arctic origin was poised to the north of Iowa.  The high gradually pushed cold air south into the central U.S. causing the precipitation on the northwest edge of the storm system to change to snow.  During the early morning hours, the system went convective with widespread "thundersnow" reported over the south half of Iowa.  Lightning struck a tree outside of a house in Nevada causing damage to electrical appliances as well as telephone damage in the house.  The "thundersnow" resulted in very heavy snowfall with a band of nine inch or greater snowfall extending from Ringgold County in the south central to Mahaska County in the southeast part of the state.  Six inches or greater fell in a several hour period over much of the southeast half of the state.  The heaviest snow was recorded in Knoxville, with 11.5 inches.  Beaconsfield came close behind with 10 inches recorded.  There was an unofficial report of 11 inches from Adair County as well.  Since the leaves were still on the trees, damage was extensive.  Numerous trees were damaged, by some estimates at least 75% of the trees.  Falling branches caused by the weight of the heavy snow felled power lines across the state.  MidAmerica Energy reported 100,000 customers were without electrical service at the height of the storm.  Fifty thousand were in the City of Des Moines.  In addition, REC's indicated another 2,000 to 3,000 people without electricity.  Damage to lines and electrical service was estimated at over $10 million.  Damage was extensive to telephone and cable TV lines as well.  Damage from those utilities was several million dollars each.  Tree damage was more difficult to assess, but was certainly several million dollars as well.  Crop damage was widespread as soybean crops in the field were virtually destroyed by the heavy wet snow.  The corn crop fared better though some lodging occurred.  At the time of the storm, 6% of the soybean crop and 51% of the corn crop remained in the field.  Damage estimates were placed at $24 million on the beans, and $41 million on the corn crop.  Following the storm, Iowa Governor Terry Branstad declared 52 Counties disaster areas.  This included Grundy, Black Hawk, Story, Marshall, Tama, Guthrie, Dallas, Polk, Jasper, Poweshiek, Cass, Adair, Madison, Warren, Marion, Mahaska, Adams, Union, Clarke, Lucas, Monroe, Wapello, Taylor, Ringgold, Decatur, Wayne, Appanoose, and Davis Counties.  Subsequently, 13 Iowa counties were declared Federal Disaster Areas.  These included Clarke, Jasper, Madison, Mahaska, Marion, Polk, Tama, Union, and Warrne Counties within the Des Moines County Warning Area.  Clean-up costs for the 13 counties was estimated at $5.1 million, ranging from $30,000 in Tama County to $1.84 million in Polk County.

Intense low pressure moved into central Canada During the early morning hours of the 13th.  A strong cold front swept through Iowa as the low lifted north.  High winds swept in as the cold air overspread the state.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 MPH were common with frequent gusts to between 50 to 60 MPH.  Scattered damage was reported over the west half of the state.  The only significant reports came from the north central counties, specifically Humboldt County.  Winds were recorded at 81 MPH near Rutland at 0347 CST.  These winds caused damage around $47,000 to a greenhouse there.  Thirty thousand dollars damage was to plants and produce.

The "thundersnow" resulted in very heavy snowfall with a band of nine inch or greater snowfall extending from Ringgold County in the south central to Mahaska County in the southeast part of the state.  Six inches or greater fell in a several hour period over much of the southeast half of the state.  The heaviest snow was recorded in Knoxville, with 11.5 inches.  Beaconsfield came close behind with 10 inches recorded.  There was an unofficial report of 11 inches from Adair County as well.  Since the leaves were still on the trees, damage was extensive.  Numerous trees were damaged, by some estimates at least 75% of the trees.  Falling branches caused by the weight of the heavy snow felled power lines across the state.  MidAmerica Energy reported 100,000 customers were without electrical service at the height of the storm.  Fifty thousand were in the City of Des Moines.  In addition, REC's indicated another 2,000 to 3,000 people without electricity.  Damage to lines and electrical service was estimated at over $10 million.  Damage was extensive to telephone and cable TV lines as well.  Damage from those utilities was several million dollars each.  Tree damage was more difficult to assess, but was certainly several million dollars as well.  Crop damage was widespread as soybean crops in the field were virtually destroyed by the heavy wet snow.  The corn crop fared better though some lodging occurred.  At the time of the storm, 6% of the soybean crop and 51% of the corn crop remained in the field.  Damage estimates were placed at $24 million on the beans, and $41 million on the corn crop.  Following the storm, Iowa Governor Terry Branstad declared 52 Counties disaster areas.  This included Grundy, Black Hawk, Story, Marshall, Tama, Guthrie, Dallas, Polk, Jasper, Poweshiek, Cass, Adair, Madison, Warren, Marion, Mahaska, Adams, Union, Clarke, Lucas, Monroe, Wapello, Taylor, Ringgold, Decatur, Wayne, Appanoose, and Davis Counties.  Subsequently, 13 Iowa counties were declared Federal Disaster Areas.  These included Clarke, Jasper, Madison, Mahaska, Marion, Polk, Tama, Union, and Warrne Counties within the Des Moines County Warning Area.  Clean-up costs for the 13 counties was estimated at $5.1 million, ranging from $30,000 in Tama County to $1.84 million in Polk County.

Iowa Gov. Terry Branstad requested disaster aid for 52 counties after an early season snowstorm dumped several inches of snow across the state.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported across the area with at least 2  serious accidents and several ditched vehicles. In Buchanan County several minor injuries were reported when a  bus went off Highway 20. One woman in Cedar Rapids was treated for injuries after she was struck by a falling tree branch.  Heavy snow downed power lines and trees and left about 120,000 persons without power across the area.  Rural Washington County residents were without 911 and long distance telephone service much of the day.  The storm closed the Cedar Rapids Airport stranding travellers for 5 hours.

Intense low pressure moved into central Canada During the early morning hours of the 13th.  A strong cold front swept through Iowa as the low lifted north.  High winds swept in as the cold air overspread the state.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 MPH were common with frequent gusts to between 50 to 60 MPH.  Scattered damage was reported over the west half of the state.  The only significant reports came from the north central counties, specifically Humboldt County.  Winds were recorded at 81 MPH near Rutland at 0347 CST.  These winds caused damage around $47,000 to a greenhouse there.  Thirty thousand dollars damage was to plants and produce.

Gradient winds caused widespread damage to trees and property in Iowa and Illinois.  Over 65,000 power customers were without power for several days due to downed power lines. A large cottonwood tree fell on a home in Camanche causing serious structural damage.  The winds slowed river operations at Lock and Dam 13 near Fulton IL as some boaters were afraid to leave the safety of the narrow channel and enter the wide open upstream pool where winds might capsize their boats.

Iowa Gov. Terry Branstad requested disaster aid for 52 counties after an early season snowstorm dumped several inches of snow across the state.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported across the area with at least 2  serious accidents and several ditched vehicles. In Buchanan County several minor injuries were reported when a  bus went off Highway 20. One woman in Cedar Rapids was treated for injuries after she was struck by a falling tree branch.  Heavy snow downed power lines and trees and left about 120,000 persons without power across the area.  Rural Washington County residents were without 911 and long distance telephone service much of the day.  The storm closed the Cedar Rapids Airport stranding travellers for 5 hours.

The first major winter storm of the season developed over the central Rockies as a powerful upper level jet stream dove into the southern Rockies.  Intense low pressure developed over northern Oklahoma, then drifted northeast on the early morning of the 26th.  A cold high pressure of Arctic origin was poised to the north of Iowa.  The high gradually pushed cold air south into the central U.S. causing the precipitation on the northwest edge of the storm system to change to snow.  During the early morning hours, the system went convective with widespread "thundersnow" reported over the south half of Iowa.  Lightning struck a tree outside of a house in Nevada causing damage to electrical appliances as well as telephone damage in the house.  The "thundersnow" resulted in very heavy snowfall with a band of nine inch or greater snowfall extending from Ringgold County in the south central to Mahaska County in the southeast part of the state.  Six inches or greater fell in a several hour period over much of the southeast half of the state.  The heaviest snow was recorded in Knoxville, with 11.5 inches.  Beaconsfield came close behind with 10 inches recorded.  There was an unofficial report of 11 inches from Adair County as well.  Since the leaves were still on the trees, damage was extensive.  Numerous trees were damaged, by some estimates at least 75% of the trees.  Falling branches caused by the weight of the heavy snow felled power lines across the state.  MidAmerica Energy reported 100,000 customers were without electrical service at the height of the storm.  Fifty thousand were in the City of Des Moines.  In addition, REC's indicated another 2,000 to 3,000 people without electricity.  Damage to lines and electrical service was estimated at over $10 million.  Damage was extensive to telephone and cable TV lines as well.  Damage from those utilities was several million dollars each.  Tree damage was more difficult to assess, but was certainly several million dollars as well.  Crop damage was widespread as soybean crops in the field were virtually destroyed by the heavy wet snow.  The corn crop fared better though some lodging occurred.  At the time of the storm, 6% of the soybean crop and 51% of the corn crop remained in the field.  Damage estimates were placed at $24 million on the beans, and $41 million on the corn crop.  Following the storm, Iowa Governor Terry Branstad declared 52 Counties disaster areas.  This included Grundy, Black Hawk, Story, Marshall, Tama, Guthrie, Dallas, Polk, Jasper, Poweshiek, Cass, Adair, Madison, Warren, Marion, Mahaska, Adams, Union, Clarke, Lucas, Monroe, Wapello, Taylor, Ringgold, Decatur, Wayne, Appanoose, and Davis Counties.  Subsequently, 13 Iowa counties were declared Federal Disaster Areas.  These included Clarke, Jasper, Madison, Mahaska, Marion, Polk, Tama, Union, and Warrne Counties within the Des Moines County Warning Area.  Clean-up costs for the 13 counties was estimated at $5.1 million, ranging from $30,000 in Tama County to $1.84 million in Polk County.

A major early season snowstorm struck portions of southwestern Iowa, blanketing the area with 6 to 9 inches of heavy wet snow.  The snow fell on trees, many of which were still fully or partially leafed, and caused extensive damage and/or total destruction.  Thousands of residents were without power just after the storm and many of the outages lasted for several days.  Emergency shelters were opened in the affected areas for use by those who suffered a hardship from the storm.

Gradient winds caused widespread damage to trees and property in Iowa and Illinois.  Over 65,000 power customers were without power for several days due to downed power lines. A large cottonwood tree fell on a home in Camanche causing serious structural damage.  The winds slowed river operations at Lock and Dam 13 near Fulton IL as some boaters were afraid to leave the safety of the narrow channel and enter the wide open upstream pool where winds might capsize their boats.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree downed at Mediapolis blocked roadway.

Cold air spilled south into Iowa as a cold polar vortex aloft dropped south into the state.  Snow fell for an extended period of snow occurred, however much of it fell during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 14th into the early morning hours of the 15th.  All of the state received snow with the heaviest over the northeast half of the state.  Amounts greater than six inches occurred over the north central counties, extending into the Waterloo area.  The heaviest snowfall report came from Butler County, where an observer in Allison reported 12 inches of snow.  Most of the snow in the heavy snow area was in the 6 to 7 inch range however.  Travel was difficult due to blowing snow and the fact that the snow initially melted on road surfaces and then froze.  Winds were generally in the 15 to 30 MPH range through the storm, causing greatly reduced visibilities in open areas.

A winter storm dumped a swath of 3 to 6 inch snowfalls across northeast Iowa and west central Illinois.  A narrow band of heavier snow produced localized amounts ranging from 6 inches in Clinton and Jackson counties to 8 inches in northern Linn county with an isolated maximum of 10 inches in northern Scott county.

Intense low pressure moved into central Canada During the early morning hours of the 13th.  A strong cold front swept through Iowa as the low lifted north.  High winds swept in as the cold air overspread the state.  Sustained winds of 30 to 40 MPH were common with frequent gusts to between 50 to 60 MPH.  Scattered damage was reported over the west half of the state.  The only significant reports came from the north central counties, specifically Humboldt County.  Winds were recorded at 81 MPH near Rutland at 0347 CST.  These winds caused damage around $47,000 to a greenhouse there.  Thirty thousand dollars damage was to plants and produce.

Gradient winds caused widespread damage to trees and property in Iowa and Illinois.  Over 65,000 power customers were without power for several days due to downed power lines. A large cottonwood tree fell on a home in Camanche causing serious structural damage.  The winds slowed river operations at Lock and Dam 13 near Fulton IL as some boaters were afraid to leave the safety of the narrow channel and enter the wide open upstream pool where winds might capsize their boats.

Iowa Gov. Terry Branstad requested disaster aid for 52 counties after an early season snowstorm dumped several inches of snow across the state.  Numerous traffic accidents were reported across the area with at least 2  serious accidents and several ditched vehicles. In Buchanan County several minor injuries were reported when a  bus went off Highway 20. One woman in Cedar Rapids was treated for injuries after she was struck by a falling tree branch.  Heavy snow downed power lines and trees and left about 120,000 persons without power across the area.  Rural Washington County residents were without 911 and long distance telephone service much of the day.  The storm closed the Cedar Rapids Airport stranding travellers for 5 hours.

A major early season snowstorm struck portions of southwestern Iowa, blanketing the area with 6 to 9 inches of heavy wet snow.  The snow fell on trees, many of which were still fully or partially leafed, and caused extensive damage and/or total destruction.  Thousands of residents were without power just after the storm and many of the outages lasted for several days.  Emergency shelters were opened in the affected areas for use by those who suffered a hardship from the storm.
High winds persisting most of the day caused tree and minor building damage.
